title: The Hallucination Problem
concept: Because LLMs are trained to predict plausible-sounding text, they occasionally invent false facts, references, or code with high confidence (hallucination).

exercises.prompt: Why do Large Language Models (LLMs) sometimes output false information that is completely Language Models (LLMs) sometimes give false facts?

options.text: They are connected to a database of verified truths.
They predict plausible-sounding text, which can sometimes be incorrect.
They are programmed to lie to users.

exercises.explain: LLMs are designed to generate fluent text based on patterns in their training data. Because they predict what sounds plausible, they can sometimes make things up.

cards.title: When AI Makes Things Up
cards.body: Have you ever talked to an AI that seemed 100% sure about a fact, only to find out later it completely made it up? It wasn't lying to you on purpose—it was experiencing a 'hallucination'. Let's learn why this happens and how to spot it!

cards.title: What is a Hallucination?
cards.body: A hallucination happens when an LLM generates text or code that is factually incorrect, fake, or non-existent, but presents it with absolute confidence. This happens because the AI's primary job is to predict what words sound right together, not to check if they are real.

cards.title: The Creative Storyteller
cards.body: Imagine a hyper-creative storyteller who has memorized every book on Earth. When you ask them a question they don't know the answer to, instead of admitting they don't know, they instantly write a highly convincing, beautiful story that sounds completely true. They aren't trying to lie; they are just doing what they do best: writing believable stories!

cards.title: A Fake Code Example
cards.body: If you ask an AI to write a Python script to convert files, it might invent a fake library called `super_fast_converter` and write perfect-looking code for it. However, if you try to install it, you will find out the library does not exist at all!

cards.title: Myth: Confidence Equals Truth
cards.body: Many people believe that if an AI sounds confident and uses proper grammar, its output must be correct. In reality, LLMs are trained to sound fluent and plausible first. A hallucinated answer can look just as professional and convincing as a correct one!

cards.title: Fact Retrieval vs. Word Prediction
cards.body: Let's compare how a human database search works versus how an LLM generates an answer.

right.text: Paints a picture by predicting words that look correct together, even if they are made up.

left.text: Looks up exact files and matches records to find real, existing facts.

options.text: When the AI gets confused and stops responding completely.
When the AI generates realistic-sounding but completely made-up facts or code.
When the AI correctly identifies a bug in your code.
When the AI copies text directly from a real website without permission.

exercises.explain: Hallucinations are incorrect or made-up facts and code generated by an AI that sound highly plausible and confident.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If an AI writes code that looks neat and uses professional terms, you can assume the code is 100% correct and safe to run without testing.
exercises.explain: Even if code looks clean, runs without syntax errors, and comes from an advanced AI, it can still contain hallucinated library names or functions. You must always test it.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to explain why AI hallucinates.
exercises.sentence: AI models hallucinate because they are trained to predict the next word, not to verify facts.
exercises.explain: LLMs are designed to predict the next most likely word in a sequence based on patterns, which sometimes leads them to invent realistic-sounding but fake details.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a classic example of an AI hallucination?

options.text: An AI refuses to answer a prompt because it violates safety guidelines.
An AI recommends a library called 'mathhelper-ultra' that cannot be found on any package manager.
An AI makes a spelling mistake in a word.

exercises.explain: AIs often hallucinate fake links, references, or software packages that look very realistic but do not exist, whereas the other options show actual knowledge or simple errors.

exercises.prompt: You ask an AI to write code using a Python library. The AI generates clean-looking code, but when you run it, you get an error because the library it used doesn't actually exist! Why did this happen?

options.text: The AI is temporarily experiencing a software virus that will soon be patched.
The AI is searching the live web and found an outdated website.
The AI predicts words that sound correct together, even if the underlying library does not exist.
The AI is trying to trick you on purpose to test your programming skills.

exercises.explain: LLMs operate by predicting the most statistically likely next word, not by double-checking facts. Therefore, they can produce fake libraries or incorrect code that looks completely valid. You must always run, test, and verify code generated by an AI before using it.
